content.content:
{
  "id": "w-mod644tf-236f3f2ecfc4",
  "type": "mandate",
  "phase": "open",
  "title": "Writ-type config schema and validator",
  "body": "# Writ-type config schema and validator\n\n## Intent\n\nEstablish the config format that governs all per-type writ lifecycles going forward. Introduce the classification and attrs meta-vocabulary, declarative state and transition definitions, and the `childrenBehavior` configuration shape. Ship TypeScript types and a static validator that checks the config's structural integrity at load time.\n\n## Motivation\n\nThis is the foundation task for the broader writ-generalization refactor. Expressing the config shape first — cleanly, in isolation — prevents shape drift once downstream work (Clerk refactor, children-behavior engine, consumer migration) begins to depend on it. Nothing else in the refactor can start until this lands.\n\n## Non-negotiable decisions\n\n- **Classification layer.** Every state declares one of three classifications: `initial`, `active`, or `terminal`. Classifications are the machine-readable layer cross-cutting infrastructure reasons over (drain detection, schedulability, terminal-event triggers). State names stay type-specific and semantically honest.\n- **Attrs layer.** States carry an optional attrs list. The v0 attrs vocabulary includes `success`, `failure`, `blocking`, and `neutral`. The validator accepts unknown attrs (forward-compatible), but the v0 consumers only react to these four.\n- **`allowedTransitions` is mandatory.** Every type declares its full state graph explicitly. Transitions not declared are rejected at runtime (enforcement lands in the Clerk refactor, not this task).\n- **`childrenBehavior` supported triggers: `allSuccess`, `anyFailure`.** No other triggers for v0 — explicitly not `anyTerminal`, `allTerminal`, or `firstChild`.\n- **`childrenBehavior` supported actions: `transition`, `copyResolution`.** No `notify` action for v0. `copyResolution` modifies a `transition` action (carries the triggering child's resolution up to the parent).\n- **`anyFailure` short-circuits `allSuccess`** when both conditions hold simultaneously.\n- **No `parentTypes` field in v0.** Parent-type constraints are deferred entirely; the data model makes no provision for them yet.\n- **No `dispatchable` field.** Dispatchability stays an emergent concern owned by each consumer (e.g., Spider decides what it will dispatch based on its own rules), not a config-level gate.\n- **Static validation at config load** must verify: referenced states exist within their type; transition-action target states are reachable from the parent's possible states via declared transitions; no duplicate state names within a type; no state with no inbound transitions (unless classified `initial`); no terminal state with outbound transitions.\n\n## Scenarios to verify\n\n- A well-formed mandate config (as it will be expressed in T2) passes validation cleanly.\n- A config with a `childrenBehavior.allSuccess: { transition: shipped }` where `shipped` is unreachable from all non-terminal states of the parent type fails validation with a clear error naming the unreachable target.\n- A config with two states sharing a name within the same type fails validation.\n- A config with an `anyFailure` trigger but no `transition` or `copyResolution` action fails validation (empty actions are not allowed).\n- A terminal state with an outbound transition fails validation.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- **Any consumer of the config** — the Clerk refactor (T2), children-behavior engine (T3), and consumer migrations (T4, T5) are separate commissions.\n- **Plugin registration surface** — `registerWritType` and related API land in T2.\n- **Runtime transition validation** — the validator here is static (load-time); per-transition enforcement is T2.\n- **Parent-type constraints** (`parentTypes`) — deferred entirely.\n- **Dispatchability metadata** — deferred entirely.\n\n## References\n\n- Parent design click: `c-mo1mqp0q` (will be concluded when the writ-generalization refactor dispatches).\n- Vision-keeper subtree (parked): `c-moa42rxh` — downstream consumer of this work.",
  "codex": "nexus",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-24T17:11:57.027Z",
  "updatedAt": "2026-04-24T19:17:24.636Z",
  "links": {
    "outbound": [],

Usage: nsg [options] [command]

Nexus Mk 2.1 — guild CLI

Options:
  --guild-root <path>                    Guild root directory (default: $GUILD_ROOT or auto-detect from cwd)
  -h, --help                             display help for command

Commands:
  init [options]                         Create a new guild — directory structure, guild.json, and package.json
  version [options]                      Show Nexus framework and installed plugin version information
  status [options]                       Show guild identity and installed plugin summary
  upgrade [options]                      Upgrade the guild framework and run pending plugin migrations
  start [options]                        Start the guild daemon (tool server + oculus + spider crawl loop)
  stop [options]                         Stop the guild daemon (graceful SIGTERM with SIGKILL escalation)
  plugin                                 plugin commands
  session                                session commands
  summon [options]                       Summon an anima — compose context and launch a session
  animator-status                        Show the Animator's current rate-limit pause state
  commission-post [options]              Post a new commission, creating a writ in open or new (draft) phase
  piece-add [options] [mandateId]        Add a new piece (atomic task) to a mandate for sequential execution
  writ                                   writ commands
  plan                                   plan commands
  inventory-write [options] [planId]     Write the codebase inventory for a plan
  scope-write [options] [planId]         Write or replace the scope items for a plan
  decisions-write [options] [planId]     Write or replace the decisions for a plan
  observations-write [options] [planId]  Write primer observations for a plan
  spec-write [options] [planId]          Write the generated specification for a plan
  codex                                  codex commands
  draft                                  draft commands
  tools                                  tools commands
  loom                                   loom commands
  oculus                                 Start the Oculus web dashboard and keep it running
  conversation                           conversation commands

  crawl                                  crawl commands
  rig                                    rig commands
  input                                  input commands
  engine-designs                         List all registered engine designs with contributing plugin info
  block-types                            List all registered block types with contributing plugin info
  help [command]                         display help for command

{

          "body": "# Writ-type config schema and validator

## Intent

Establish the config format that governs all per-type writ lifecycles going forward. Introduce the classification and attrs meta-vocabulary, declarative state and transition definitions, and the `childrenBehavior` configuration shape. Ship TypeScript types and a static validator that checks the config's structural integrity at load time.

## Motivation

This is the foundation task for the broader writ-generalization refactor. Expressing the config shape first — cleanly, in isolation — prevents shape drift once downstream work (Clerk refactor, children-behavior engine, consumer migration) begins to depend on it. Nothing else in the refactor can start until this lands.

## Non-negotiable decisions

- **Classification layer.** Every state declares one of three classifications: `initial`, `active`, or `terminal`. Classifications are the machine-readable layer cross-cutting infrastructure reasons over (drain detection, schedulability, terminal-event triggers). State names stay type-specific and semantically honest.
- **Attrs layer.** States carry an optional attrs list. The v0 attrs vocabulary includes `success`, `failure`, `blocking`, and `neutral`. The validator accepts unknown attrs (forward-compatible), but the v0 consumers only react to these four.
- **`allowedTransitions` is mandatory.** Every type declares its full state graph explicitly. Transitions not declared are rejected at runtime (enforcement lands in the Clerk refactor, not this task).
- **`childrenBehavior` supported triggers: `allSuccess`, `anyFailure`.** No other triggers for v0 — explicitly not `anyTerminal`, `allTerminal`, or `firstChild`.
- **`childrenBehavior` supported actions: `transition`, `copyResolution`.** No `notify` action for v0. `copyResolution` modifies a `transition` action (carries the triggering child's resolution up to the parent).
- **`anyFailure` short-circuits `allSuccess`** when both conditions hold simultaneously.
- **No `parentTypes` field in v0.** Parent-type constraints are deferred entirely; the data model makes no provision for them yet.
- **No `dispatchable` field.** Dispatchability stays an emergent concern owned by each consumer (e.g., Spider decides what it will dispatch based on its own rules), not a config-level gate.
- **Static validation at config load** must verify: referenced states exist within their type; transition-action target states are reachable from the parent's possible states via declared transitions; no duplicate state names within a type; no state with no inbound transitions (unless classified `initial`); no terminal state with outbound transitions.

## Scenarios to verify

- A well-formed mandate config (as it will be expressed in T2) passes validation cleanly.
- A config with a `childrenBehavior.allSuccess: { transition: shipped }` where `shipped` is unreachable from all non-terminal states of the parent type fails validation with a clear error naming the unreachable target.
- A config with two states sharing a name within the same type fails validation.
- A config with an `anyFailure` trigger but no `transition` or `copyResolution` action fails validation (empty actions are not allowed).
- A terminal state with an outbound transition fails validation.

## Out of scope

- **Any consumer of the config** — the Clerk refactor (T2), children-behavior engine (T3), and consumer migrations (T4, T5) are separate commissions.
- **Plugin registration surface** — `registerWritType` and related API land in T2.
- **Runtime transition validation** — the validator here is static (load-time); per-transition enforcement is T2.
- **Parent-type constraints** (`parentTypes`) — deferred entirely.
- **Dispatchability metadata** — deferred entirely.

## References

- Parent design click: `c-mo1mqp0q` (will be concluded when the writ-generalization refactor dispatches).
- Vision-keeper subtree (parked): `c-moa42rxh` — downstream consumer of this work.",

- Post-section uses the `.card` class All 164 tests pass.(node:1676133) ExperimentalWarning: Transform Types is an experimental feature and might change at any time (Use `node --trace-warnings ...` to show where the warning was created) Let me examine the current state of the files and compare with reference pages.Now let me check indentation consistency in the card wrappers:(node:1676710) ExperimentalWarning: Transform Types is an experimental feature and might change at any time (Use `node --trace-warnings ...` to show where the warning was created) I have all the information needed. Here are my findings: --- ### Overall: PASS ### Completeness - ✅ **Viewport-filling layout fixed**: Page content is now wrapped in a `
` element with `padding: 24px`, matching the pattern used by Spider, Loom/Guild, and Animator pages. - ✅ **Page heading added**: An `

Writs

` heading is present, consistent with other pages. - ✅ **Card treatment applied**: The toolbar, post-section form, and data table are each wrapped in `.card` containers, matching the visual grouping pattern used elsewhere. - ✅ **Consistent spacing**: `margin-bottom: 16px` is used between card sections, matching the established convention. ### Correctness - ✅ Build passes. - ✅ All tests pass. - No logic errors or regressions — the changes are purely structural/presentational HTML wrapping with no impact on the JavaScript behavior. - The `#post-section` style was simplified (removed `padding`, `background`, `border-radius`) since the `.card` class now provides those properties — this is correct. - The `#load-more-row` was moved outside the table card, which is reasonable since it's a pagination control, though its inline `text-align: center; margin-top: 8px` duplicates the existing CSS rule on line 42 (`#load-more-row { text-align: center; padding: 0.75rem; }`). This is minor and non-breaking. ### Quality - **Indentation is inconsistent**: The children of the new `.card` wrappers are not indented relative to their card parent. For example, on lines 50–66 the `.toolbar` div sits at the same indentation level as its `.card` wrapper, and on lines 98–112 the `` similarly lacks indentation inside its card. Other pages (Spider, Loom) indent children within cards. This is a cosmetic HTML style issue, not a functional one. - **Test coverage is appropriate**: Four new tests verify the key structural changes (`
` with padding, `

`, card class count ≥ 2, post-section card). Tests are straightforward string-based checks on the HTML file, consistent with the existing test style in this suite. - **`

{
  "id": "implement",
  "designId": "implement",
  "status": "running",
  "upstream": [
    "observation-lift"
  ],
  "givensSpec": {
    "writ": {
      "id": "w-mod644tf-236f3f2ecfc4",
      "type": "mandate",
      "phase": "open",
      "title": "Writ-type config schema and validator",
      "body": "# Writ-type config schema and validator

## Intent

Establish the config format that governs all per-type writ lifecycles going forward. Introduce the classification and attrs meta-vocabulary, declarative state and transition definitions, and the `childrenBehavior` configuration shape. Ship TypeScript types and a static validator that checks the config's structural integrity at load time.

## Motivation

This is the foundation task for the broader writ-generalization refactor. Expressing the config shape first — cleanly, in isolation — prevents shape drift once downstream work (Clerk refactor, children-behavior engine, consumer migration) begins to depend on it. Nothing else in the refactor can start until this lands.

## Non-negotiable decisions

- **Classification layer.** Every state declares one of three classifications: `initial`, `active`, or `terminal`. Classifications are the machine-readable layer cross-cutting infrastructure reasons over (drain detection, schedulability, terminal-event triggers). State names stay type-specific and semantically honest.
- **Attrs layer.** States carry an optional attrs list. The v0 attrs vocabulary includes `success`, `failure`, `blocking`, and `neutral`. The validator accepts unknown attrs (forward-compatible), but the v0 consumers only react to these four.
- **`allowedTransitions` is mandatory.** Every type declares its full state graph explicitly. Transitions not declared are rejected at runtime (enforcement lands in the Clerk refactor, not this task).
- **`childrenBehavior` supported triggers: `allSuccess`, `anyFailure`.** No other triggers for v0 — explicitly not `anyTerminal`, `allTerminal`, or `firstChild`.
- **`childrenBehavior` supported actions: `transition`, `copyResolution`.** No `notify` action for v0. `copyResolution` modifies a `transition` action (carries the triggering child's resolution up to the parent).
- **`anyFailure` short-circuits `allSuccess`** when both conditions hold simultaneously.
- **No `parentTypes` field in v0.** Parent-type constraints are deferred entirely; the data model makes no provision for them yet.
- **No `dispatchable` field.** Dispatchability stays an emergent concern owned by each consumer (e.g., Spider decides what it will dispatch based on its own rules), not a config-level gate.
- **Static validation at config load** must verify: referenced states exist within their type; transition-action target states are reachable from the parent's possible states via declared transitions; no duplicate state names within a type; no state with no inbound transitions (unless classified `initial`); no terminal state with outbound transitions.

## Scenarios to verify

- A well-formed mandate config (as it will be expressed in T2) passes validation cleanly.
- A config with a `childrenBehavior.allSuccess: { transition: shipped }` where `shipped` is unreachable from all non-terminal states of the parent type fails validation with a clear error naming the unreachable target.
- A config with two states sharing a name within the same type fails validation.
- A config with an `anyFailure` trigger but no `transition` or `copyResolution` action fails validation (empty actions are not allowed).
- A terminal state with an outbound transition fails validation.

## Out of scope

- **Any consumer of the config** — the Clerk refactor (T2), children-behavior engine (T3), and consumer migrations (T4, T5) are separate commissions.
- **Plugin registration surface** — `registerWritType` and related API land in T2.
- **Runtime transition validation** — the validator here is static (load-time); per-transition enforcement is T2.
- **Parent-type constraints** (`parentTypes`) — deferred entirely.
- **Dispatchability metadata** — deferred entirely.

## References

- Parent design click: `c-mo1mqp0q` (will be concluded when the writ-generalization refactor dispatches).
- Vision-keeper subtree (parked): `c-moa42rxh` — downstream consumer of this work.",

      "createdAt": "2026-04-24T17:11:57.027Z",
      "updatedAt": "2026-04-24T17:14:20.343Z"

- **Attrs layer.** States carry an optional attrs list. The v0 attrs vocabulary includes `success`, `failure`, `blocking`, and `neutral`. The validator accepts unknown attrs (forward-compatible), but the v0 consumers only react to these four.
- **`allowedTransitions` is mandatory.** Every type declares its full state graph explicitly. Transitions not declared are rejected at runtime (enforcement lands in the Clerk refactor, not this task).
- **`childrenBehavior` supported triggers: `allSuccess`, `anyFailure`.** No other triggers for v0 — explicitly not `anyTerminal`, `allTerminal`, or `firstChild`.
- **`childrenBehavior` supported actions: `transition`, `copyResolution`.** No `notify` action for v0. `copyResolution` modifies a `transition` action (carries the triggering child's resolution up to the parent).
- **`anyFailure` short-circuits `allSuccess`** when both conditions hold simultaneously.
- **No `parentTypes` field in v0.** Parent-type constraints are deferred entirely; the data model makes no provision for them yet.
- **No `dispatchable` field.** Dispatchability stays an emergent concern owned by each consumer (e.g., Spider decides what it will dispatch based on its own rules), not a config-level gate.
- **Static validation at config load** must verify: referenced states exist within their type; transition-action target states are reachable from the parent's possible states via declared transitions; no duplicate state names within a type; no state with no inbound transitions (unless classified `initial`); no terminal state with outbound transitions.

input.description: Find where engine attempts are written

lastPrompt: rig seems hung --     rig-mod69jd9-3d0c607f ... please put the current job back into a pending state or w/e so spider picks it up. then try to do an analysis of what caused the hang

content.content:
30	 *
31	 * There is no `'blocked'` value: holds are `'pending'` with hold metadata.
32	 */
33	export type EngineStatus = 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled' | 'skipped';
34	
35	// ── Engine attempt history ────────────────────────────────────────────
36	
37	/**
38	 * A single engine attempt — one dispatch of the engine's run(), from
39	 * the moment the dispatcher picks it up through its terminal outcome.
40	 *
41	 * Entries are append-on-start: tryRun pushes a row with `startedAt` when
42	 * dispatching, and the terminal handler (or the success path) patches the
43	 * tail row with `endedAt`, `status`, and `error`/`yields` at completion.
44	 *
45	 * Scalar engine-level `startedAt`/`completedAt`/`error`/`sessionId`/`yields`
46	 * fields do not exist — `attempts[-1]` is authoritative.
47	 */
48	export interface EngineAttempt {
49	  /** ISO timestamp when the attempt started. */
50	  startedAt: string;
51	  /** ISO timestamp when the attempt terminated; absent while in-flight. */
52	  endedAt?: string;
53	  /**
54	   * Terminal attempt status — only the two terminal outcomes a single
55	   * attempt can reach. `'completed'` means the attempt produced yields;
56	   * `'failed'` means the attempt threw or observed a non-rate-limit
57	   * session terminal. Absent while the attempt is still in-flight.
58	   */
59	  status?: 'completed' | 'failed';
60	  /** Error message if the attempt terminated in `'failed'`. */
61	  error?: string;
62	  /** Animator session id associated with this attempt, if any. */
63	  sessionId?: string;
64	  /** Yields produced by this attempt, if `status === 'completed'`. */
65	  yields?: unknown;
66	}
67	
68	// ── Engine instance ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
69	
70	/**
71	 * A single engine slot within a rig.
72	 *
73	 * `id` is the engine's position identifier (e.g. 'draft', 'implement').
74	 * For the static pipeline it matches `designId`.
75	 *
76	 * `givensSpec` holds values set at spawn time (writ, role, commands) and
77	 * may contain unresolved yield expression strings (`${yields.<id>.<path>}`)
78	 * that the Spider resolves at run time from upstream engine yields.
79	 *
80	 * Hold metadata (`holdUntil`, `holdReason`, `holdCondition`, `lastCheckedAt`)
81	 * is present while the engine is in `'pending'` due to a retry back-off
82	 * window or an external-gate BlockType. It is cleared when the hold is
83	 * resolved (by poll-clear, by window expiration, or by operator resume).
84	 *
85	 * `attempts[]` is the append-only per-dispatch history for this engine.
86	 * The latest entry carries the in-flight or most recently completed
87	 * attempt's timestamps, session id, yields, and error.
88	 */
89	export interface EngineInstance {
90	  /** Unique identifier within the rig (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
91	  id: string;
92	  /** The engine design to look up in the Fabricator. */
93	  designId: string;
94	  /** Current execution status. */
95	  status: EngineStatus;
96	  /** Engine IDs that must be completed before this engine can run. */
97	  upstream: string[];
98	  /**
99	   * Givens values. Spawn-time expressions (`${writ}`, `${writ.*}`, `${vars.*}`)
100	   * are resolved to their values. Yield expressions (`${yields.*}`) remain as
101	   * literal `${yields.*}` strings and are resolved at run time when the engine
102	   * is executed.
103	   */
104	  givensSpec: Record<string, unknown>;
105	  /**
106	   * Conditional activation expression, copied from the template.
107	   * Evaluated at runtime when upstream is all done. Absent means unconditional.
108	   */
109	  when?: string;
110	  /**
111	   * Per-dispatch history. Each entry records one attempt's lifecycle.
112	   * Downstream code reads `attempts[attempts.length - 1]` for the latest
113	   * state (sessionId, yields, error). An empty array means the engine
114	   * has never been dispatched.
115	   */
116	  attempts?: EngineAttempt[];
117	  /**
118	   * Retry-budget counter. Incremented only when the failure handler
119	   * routes a terminal attempt to the retryable-within-budget branch. A
120	   * rate-limit hold does not increment; a terminal-failed outcome does
121	   * not increment (it records the final consumed attempt via attempts[]).
122	   */
123	  attemptCount?: number;
124	  /**
125	   * ISO timestamp this engine may not dispatch before. Set by the retry
126	   * back-off path; also set by BlockTypes whose hold carries a natural
127	   * deadline. When undefined, the hold is purely gate-driven (the
128	   * BlockType's `check()` result decides readiness).
129	   */
130	  holdUntil?: string;
131	  /**
132	   * BlockType id describing why the engine is being held. When set to a
133	   * value registered in the block type registry, the dispatch predicate
134	   * delegates to that BlockType's `check()` for external-gate
135	   * evaluation. The internal sentinel `'retry-backoff'` is purely
136	   * timer-driven and is not registered in the registry — the predicate
137	   * relies on `holdUntil` alone to clear it. Well-known registered
138	   * values include `'animator-paused'` (the rate-limit gate — the
139	   * engine-failure path writes this id when a session reports a
140	   * rate-limit terminal), `'writ-phase'`, `'scheduled-time'`,
141	   * `'patron-input'`, and `'book-updated'`.
142	   */
143	  holdReason?: string;
144	  /**
145	   * Structured payload validated by the BlockType's `conditionSchema`.
146	   * Carries the specifics needed by `check()` (e.g. `{ sessionId }` for
147	   * `animator-paused`). Shape is opaque to the Spider.
148	   */
149	  holdCondition?: unknown;
150	  /**
151	   * ISO timestamp of the last dispatch-predicate check on this hold.
152	   * The predicate honours the BlockType's `pollIntervalMs` against this
153	   * stamp — `check()` only re-runs after the interval elapses.
154	   */
155	  lastCheckedAt?: string;
156	}
157	
158	// ── Rig ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
159

content.text:
Now I understand the problem. Let me brief you before acting:

**Context**
- Rig `rig-mod69jd9-3d0c607f` has engine `implement` stuck at `running`. Its session (`ses-modd2fs5-1f17cc92`) actually died at 20:31:11 — reconciled as "No heartbeat received for 126s — session host presumed dead". The engine status was never updated.
- Current time is 21:07; daemon has been crawling since 20:32.

**Root cause (schema drift, I think)**
- The engine doc has scalar fields `sessionId` and `startedAt` at the engine level, and **no `attempts[]` array**. Every engine in this rig is shaped this way.
- Current Spider code (`tryCollect`, spider.ts:1723) reads the session via `latestAttempt(engine)?.sessionId` — which only looks at `engine.attempts[-1].sessionId`. With `attempts` missing, `sessionId` is `undefined`, so the engine is silently skipped every crawl tick.
- The type declaration explicitly says "Scalar engine-level `startedAt`/`completedAt`/`error`/`sessionId`/`yields` fields do not exist — `attempts[-1]` is authoritative." But the DB still has the scalars on a rig created at 17:16 today.
- Completed engines in this rig (`reader-analyst`, `patron-anima`, `spec-writer`) also have scalar `sessionId`/no `attempts` — meaning when they finished, the old-schema-compatible path ran. At some point between those completions and implement's dispatch, something changed such that the scalar-sessionId reader fell off.

Recovery: reset `implement` status to `pending` and clear the scalar fields; Spider will re-dispatch with a fresh attempt. Backing up first.

      "body": "# Writ-type config schema and validator

## Intent

Establish the config format that governs all per-type writ lifecycles going forward. Introduce the classification and attrs meta-vocabulary, declarative state and transition definitions, and the `childrenBehavior` configuration shape. Ship TypeScript types and a static validator that checks the config's structural integrity at load time.

## Motivation

This is the foundation task for the broader writ-generalization refactor. Expressing the config shape first — cleanly, in isolation — prevents shape drift once downstream work (Clerk refactor, children-behavior engine, consumer migration) begins to depend on it. Nothing else in the refactor can start until this lands.

## Non-negotiable decisions

- **Classification layer.** Every state declares one of three classifications: `initial`, `active`, or `terminal`. Classifications are the machine-readable layer cross-cutting infrastructure reasons over (drain detection, schedulability, terminal-event triggers). State names stay type-specific and semantically honest.
- **Attrs layer.** States carry an optional attrs list. The v0 attrs vocabulary includes `success`, `failure`, `blocking`, and `neutral`. The validator accepts unknown attrs (forward-compatible), but the v0 consumers only react to these four.
- **`allowedTransitions` is mandatory.** Every type declares its full state graph explicitly. Transitions not declared are rejected at runtime (enforcement lands in the Clerk refactor, not this task).
- **`childrenBehavior` supported triggers: `allSuccess`, `anyFailure`.** No other triggers for v0 — explicitly not `anyTerminal`, `allTerminal`, or `firstChild`.
- **`childrenBehavior` supported actions: `transition`, `copyResolution`.** No `notify` action for v0. `copyResolution` modifies a `transition` action (carries the triggering child's resolution up to the parent).
- **`anyFailure` short-circuits `allSuccess`** when both conditions hold simultaneously.
- **No `parentTypes` field in v0.** Parent-type constraints are deferred entirely; the data model makes no provision for them yet.
- **No `dispatchable` field.** Dispatchability stays an emergent concern owned by each consumer (e.g., Spider decides what it will dispatch based on its own rules), not a config-level gate.
- **Static validation at config load** must verify: referenced states exist within their type; transition-action target states are reachable from the parent's possible states via declared transitions; no duplicate state names within a type; no state with no inbound transitions (unless classified `initial`); no terminal state with outbound transitions.

## Scenarios to verify

- A well-formed mandate config (as it will be expressed in T2) passes validation cleanly.
- A config with a `childrenBehavior.allSuccess: { transition: shipped }` where `shipped` is unreachable from all non-terminal states of the parent type fails validation with a clear error naming the unreachable target.
- A config with two states sharing a name within the same type fails validation.
- A config with an `anyFailure` trigger but no `transition` or `copyResolution` action fails validation (empty actions are not allowed).
- A terminal state with an outbound transition fails validation.

## Out of scope

- **Any consumer of the config** — the Clerk refactor (T2), children-behavior engine (T3), and consumer migrations (T4, T5) are separate commissions.
- **Plugin registration surface** — `registerWritType` and related API land in T2.
- **Runtime transition validation** — the validator here is static (load-time); per-transition enforcement is T2.
- **Parent-type constraints** (`parentTypes`) — deferred entirely.
- **Dispatchability metadata** — deferred entirely.

## References

- Parent design click: `c-mo1mqp0q` (will be concluded when the writ-generalization refactor dispatches).
- Vision-keeper subtree (parked): `c-moa42rxh` — downstream consumer of this work.",

- A config with an `anyFailure` trigger but no `transition` or `copyResolution` action fails validation (empty actions are not allowed).
- A terminal state with an outbound transition fails validation.

## Out of scope

- **Any consumer of the config** — the Clerk refactor (T2), children-behavior engine (T3), and consumer migrations (T4, T5) are separate commissions.
- **Plugin registration surface** — `registerWritType` and related API land in T2.
- **Runtime transition validation** — the validator here is static (load-time); per-transition enforcement is T2.
- **Parent-type constraints** (`parentTypes`) — deferred entirely.
- **Dispatchability metadata** — deferred entirely.

    "spec": "# Writ-type config schema and validator

## Intent

Ship the TypeScript types that describe a writ-type configuration and a pure static validator that enforces its structural integrity. This is the foundation of the broader writ-generalization refactor: every downstream task (Clerk refactor, children-behavior engine, consumer migrations) will import these types, and every plugin-registered writ type will pass through this validator before becoming usable.

## Rationale

Expressing the config shape cleanly and in isolation — before any consumer depends on it — prevents shape drift across the multi-task refactor. A downstream task that discovers a missing field or an awkward shape after the fact must propagate changes through every sibling commission; isolating the schema to this task removes that risk. The validator is equally important: without a runtime check at config load, downstream consumers cannot trust any plugin-supplied type configuration.

## Scope & Blast Radius

This task is greenfield and narrow. Two new files inside `packages/plugins/clerk/src/` (one module for the types and validator, one sibling test file) plus additive re-exports in the Clerk package barrel (`packages/plugins/clerk/src/index.ts`).

Out of scope — deliberately untouched:

- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts` — the Clerk factory, including the existing `ALLOWED_FROM`, `TERMINAL_PHASES`, and child-cascade handlers. A later task consumes this validator; this task must not pre-empt it.
- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` — `WritDoc`, `WritPhase`, `ClerkConfig`, `WritTypeEntry` all stay as-is. The new types live in a sibling module.
- Any `GuildConfig` module augmentation. No new guild.json-side schema is introduced here.
- The plugin registration API (`registerWritType`) — ownership of that surface belongs to a later task.
- Every downstream consumer (Spider, Astrolabe, Reckoner, CLI, Oculus pages).

No cross-cutting blast radius: by construction, nothing outside the Clerk package imports anything that doesn't yet exist.

## Decisions

| # | Decision | Default | Rationale |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|
| D1 | Where do the new types and validator live? | Co-located under `packages/plugins/clerk/src/` as a sibling module to `link-normalize.ts`, with a sibling `.test.ts`. | Matches the established single-pure-function precedent. One file pair does not earn a subdirectory or a shared package. |
| D2 | Top-level config type name | `WritTypeConfig` | Matches the `*Config` suffix convention (`ClerkConfig`, `AnimatorRateLimitBackoffConfig`) and the brief's phrasing. |
| D3 | Shape of the state collection on a config | Array of state objects — each object carries its own `name`. | The duplicate-state-name check is only meaningful if duplicates are representable. A map keyed by name would silently collapse duplicates, hiding a class of config bugs. |
| D4 | Where `allowedTransitions` attaches | On each state object (per-state), not as a top-level matrix. | Every state must declare its outbound edges; per-state placement keeps the shape self-contained and localizes the "must be present" check. |
| D5 | Shape of `childrenBehavior` | Named-field object with optional `allSuccess` and `anyFailure` keys. | The v0 trigger set is exactly two; named fields communicate exhaustiveness. Adding a future trigger is a pure additive change. |
| D6 | How `copyResolution` attaches to a `transition` action | As an optional boolean flag sitting on the action object next to the `transition` target. | The brief calls `copyResolution` a modifier on `transition`; a flag-on-action encodes that literally. |
| D7 | Representation of `classification` | String-literal union (`'initial' \| 'active' \| 'terminal'`). | Matches every sibling vocabulary in the writ/state domain. Enums are not used here. |
| D8 | How `attrs` is typed given forward-compatibility | A known-union extended with an open string (so known values autocomplete but unknown strings are accepted). Ship a named type for the known-attrs union. | Preserves the brief's forward-compatibility requirement while giving consumers editor autocomplete on the four documented attrs. |
| D9 | How the validator reports errors | Throws a plain `Error` on the first structural violation. No typed error class, no Result object. | Matches the one in-tree precedent (`validateBackoffConfig`). Fail-loud default; no accumulated-error complexity. |
| D10 | Does the validator return a resolved/normalized config? | No — throw-only, returning `void`. | No meaningful defaults to fill in; adding normalization without a consumer bloats the API. |
| D11 | Semantics of "transition-action target is reachable" | Target must be reachable from *every* non-terminal state of the same type via `allowedTransitions`. | The brief's scenario spells out all-reachability; looser semantics would let configs ship with a cascade target the parent can never reach from some states. |
| D12 | Initial-state count constraint | Exactly one state per type must carry `classification: 'initial'`. | Zero breaks writ creation; multiple make the creation choice ambiguous. Every sibling state machine in-tree has exactly one starting state. |
| D13 | Depth of writ-type `name` validation | Non-empty string only. No format rules (kebab-case, etc.). | The brief lists the structural checks; name format is not among them. Cross-type uniqueness is a registration-layer concern owned by a later task. |
| D14 | Where the "well-formed mandate config" test fixture lives | Inline in the test file. No shared fixture exported. | Shipping a canonical mandate fixture would make this task a de facto consumer of its own config, pre-empting the Clerk refactor. |
| D15 | Action-presence check on triggers | Every declared trigger must carry an action with a `transition` field. Empty action objects and transition-less actions are rejected. | `copyResolution` is a modifier on `transition`, not a standalone action. Matches the brief's empty-action scenario. |
| D16 | Does this task add any `GuildConfig` module augmentation? | No. Guild.json-side schema is untouched. | Registration path is owned by a later task; pre-empting it here would cross scope boundaries. |
| D17 | Error message format | Mirror the animator precedent: `[clerk] writTypeConfig.<path>: <problem>; received <value>`. | The only established in-tree precedent for structured config-validation errors. Stable, path-based messages make tests straightforward. |
| D18 | Does the validator detect cycles in the state graph? | No. Cycles among non-terminal states are permitted. | Mandate's `open ↔ stuck` is a legitimate cycle; cycle rejection would break the canonical config. Not listed among the brief's required checks. |

## Acceptance Signal

- The Clerk package's test suite passes — run `pnpm --filter @shardworks/clerk-apparatus test` (or the repo-wide `pnpm -w test`); the new sibling test file runs alongside the existing Clerk tests.
- The Clerk package typechecks cleanly — run `pnpm -w typecheck`.
- A well-formed mandate-shaped config (six states: `new`, `open`, `stuck`, `completed`, `failed`, `cancelled`, with the transition graph that mirrors the existing `ALLOWED_FROM` and a `childrenBehavior` declaring both `allSuccess` and `anyFailure` triggers) passes the validator without throwing.
- Each of the five brief-specified failure scenarios (unreachable `childrenBehavior` target from some non-terminal state; duplicate state names; empty/transition-less action on a declared trigger; terminal state with an outbound transition; well-formed config passing) has a corresponding test asserting the validator's behavior, and each failure-case test asserts the error message contains the offending field path.
- Each of the additional structural checks (referenced states must exist; non-initial states must have at least one inbound transition; exactly one initial state; non-empty type name) has at least one failing-case test.
- The validator throws on the *first* structural violation (no accumulated-error mode).
- The new types and the validator are importable from `@shardworks/clerk-apparatus` at the package's public entry point; no consumer needs to reach into an internal path.

## Existing Patterns

- **Canonical validator shape.** `packages/plugins/animator/src/rate-limit-backoff.ts::validateBackoffConfig` is the single closest precedent: pure function, structural checks layered top-down, plain-Error throws with the `[apparatus] path: problem; received value` message shape, accompanied by a sibling `.test.ts` with one happy-path plus one failing test per rejection mode. Follow this shape with no variation.
- **File layout precedent.** `packages/plugins/clerk/src/link-normalize.ts` and its sibling `link-normalize.test.ts` are the shape to mirror: one pure-function module co-located with its test, re-exported from the package barrel.
- **Kit-contribution validation idiom.** `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts::registerKitLinkKinds` shows how this codebase layers structural checks on plugin-contributed config entries and emits `[clerk] ...`-prefixed errors. Error-message voice should match.
- **Reachability implementation hint.** The all-reachability check (decision D11) is one forward-BFS per distinct transition target. Computing the forward-reachability closure once per starting state (or using a single-source reachability over the reversed graph from each target) is sufficient; a naive implementation nesting BFS inside a per-state loop works correctly but is slower than necessary. This is a correctness-preserving style note, not a requirement.
- **Existing state-machine vocabularies.** Read `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` (`WritPhase`, `WritCrawlResult`) and the state declarations in `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts` and `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/types.ts` for tone and naming — all three use string-literal unions. Stay consistent.

## What NOT To Do

- Do not modify `clerk.ts`, `types.ts`, or `guild-config.ts`. The refactor's other tasks own those files.
- Do not register the built-in mandate type. A later task owns that registration; duplicating the canonical mandate config here (for example as a shared export or a constant used outside the test file) pre-empts it. Inline the fixture in the test file only.
- Do not introduce a typed error class or a `Result`-style return. The validator throws plain `Error` objects and is `void`-returning on success.
- Do not add normalization, default-filling, or a "resolved config" return shape. The validator is pure validation.
- Do not enforce writ-type-`name` format rules (kebab-case, reserved-word lists, etc.). Non-empty is the only name-level check in this task.
- Do not detect cycles in the state graph. Mandate's `open ↔ stuck` is a legitimate cycle and rejecting cycles would break the canonical test case.
- Do not examine or modify `ClerkConfig.writTypes`. The guild.json-side `WritTypeEntry[]` shape is superseded by later work but is not this task's concern.
- Do not lift the new types or validator into a new shared workspace package. Colocation inside the Clerk package is the correct destination.
- Do not add JSDoc-deprecation markers to existing types or fields.

  "status": "completed",
  "yields": {
    "spec": "# Writ-type config schema and validator

## Intent

Ship the TypeScript types that describe a writ-type configuration and a pure static validator that enforces its structural integrity. This is the foundation of the broader writ-generalization refactor: every downstream task (Clerk refactor, children-behavior engine, consumer migrations) will import these types, and every plugin-registered writ type will pass through this validator before becoming usable.

## Rationale

Expressing the config shape cleanly and in isolation — before any consumer depends on it — prevents shape drift across the multi-task refactor. A downstream task that discovers a missing field or an awkward shape after the fact must propagate changes through every sibling commission; isolating the schema to this task removes that risk. The validator is equally important: without a runtime check at config load, downstream consumers cannot trust any plugin-supplied type configuration.

## Scope & Blast Radius

This task is greenfield and narrow. Two new files inside `packages/plugins/clerk/src/` (one module for the types and validator, one sibling test file) plus additive re-exports in the Clerk package barrel (`packages/plugins/clerk/src/index.ts`).

Out of scope — deliberately untouched:

- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts` — the Clerk factory, including the existing `ALLOWED_FROM`, `TERMINAL_PHASES`, and child-cascade handlers. A later task consumes this validator; this task must not pre-empt it.
- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` — `WritDoc`, `WritPhase`, `ClerkConfig`, `WritTypeEntry` all stay as-is. The new types live in a sibling module.
- Any `GuildConfig` module augmentation. No new guild.json-side schema is introduced here.
- The plugin registration API (`registerWritType`) — ownership of that surface belongs to a later task.
- Every downstream consumer (Spider, Astrolabe, Reckoner, CLI, Oculus pages).

No cross-cutting blast radius: by construction, nothing outside the Clerk package imports anything that doesn't yet exist.

## Decisions

| # | Decision | Default | Rationale |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|
| D1 | Where do the new types and validator live? | Co-located under `packages/plugins/clerk/src/` as a sibling module to `link-normalize.ts`, with a sibling `.test.ts`. | Matches the established single-pure-function precedent. One file pair does not earn a subdirectory or a shared package. |
| D2 | Top-level config type name | `WritTypeConfig` | Matches the `*Config` suffix convention (`ClerkConfig`, `AnimatorRateLimitBackoffConfig`) and the brief's phrasing. |
| D3 | Shape of the state collection on a config | Array of state objects — each object carries its own `name`. | The duplicate-state-name check is only meaningful if duplicates are representable. A map keyed by name would silently collapse duplicates, hiding a class of config bugs. |
| D4 | Where `allowedTransitions` attaches | On each state object (per-state), not as a top-level matrix. | Every state must declare its outbound edges; per-state placement keeps the shape self-contained and localizes the "must be present" check. |
| D5 | Shape of `childrenBehavior` | Named-field object with optional `allSuccess` and `anyFailure` keys. | The v0 trigger set is exactly two; named fields communicate exhaustiveness. Adding a future trigger is a pure additive change. |
| D6 | How `copyResolution` attaches to a `transition` action | As an optional boolean flag sitting on the action object next to the `transition` target. | The brief calls `copyResolution` a modifier on `transition`; a flag-on-action encodes that literally. |
| D7 | Representation of `classification` | String-literal union (`'initial' \| 'active' \| 'terminal'`). | Matches every sibling vocabulary in the writ/state domain. Enums are not used here. |
| D8 | How `attrs` is typed given forward-compatibility | A known-union extended with an open string (so known values autocomplete but unknown strings are accepted). Ship a named type for the known-attrs union. | Preserves the brief's forward-compatibility requirement while giving consumers editor autocomplete on the four documented attrs. |
| D9 | How the validator reports errors | Throws a plain `Error` on the first structural violation. No typed error class, no Result object. | Matches the one in-tree precedent (`validateBackoffConfig`). Fail-loud default; no accumulated-error complexity. |
| D10 | Does the validator return a resolved/normalized config? | No — throw-only, returning `void`. | No meaningful defaults to fill in; adding normalization without a consumer bloats the API. |
| D11 | Semantics of "transition-action target is reachable" | Target must be reachable from *every* non-terminal state of the same type via `allowedTransitions`. | The brief's scenario spells out all-reachability; looser semantics would let configs ship with a cascade target the parent can never reach from some states. |
| D12 | Initial-state count constraint | Exactly one state per type must carry `classification: 'initial'`. | Zero breaks writ creation; multiple make the creation choice ambiguous. Every sibling state machine in-tree has exactly one starting state. |
| D13 | Depth of writ-type `name` validation | Non-empty string only. No format rules (kebab-case, etc.). | The brief lists the structural checks; name format is not among them. Cross-type uniqueness is a registration-layer concern owned by a later task. |
| D14 | Where the "well-formed mandate config" test fixture lives | Inline in the test file. No shared fixture exported. | Shipping a canonical mandate fixture would make this task a de facto consumer of its own config, pre-empting the Clerk refactor. |
| D15 | Action-presence check on triggers | Every declared trigger must carry an action with a `transition` field. Empty action objects and transition-less actions are rejected. | `copyResolution` is a modifier on `transition`, not a standalone action. Matches the brief's empty-action scenario. |
| D16 | Does this task add any `GuildConfig` module augmentation? | No. Guild.json-side schema is untouched. | Registration path is owned by a later task; pre-empting it here would cross scope boundaries. |
| D17 | Error message format | Mirror the animator precedent: `[clerk] writTypeConfig.<path>: <problem>; received <value>`. | The only established in-tree precedent for structured config-validation errors. Stable, path-based messages make tests straightforward. |
| D18 | Does the validator detect cycles in the state graph? | No. Cycles among non-terminal states are permitted. | Mandate's `open ↔ stuck` is a legitimate cycle; cycle rejection would break the canonical config. Not listed among the brief's required checks. |

## Acceptance Signal

- The Clerk package's test suite passes — run `pnpm --filter @shardworks/clerk-apparatus test` (or the repo-wide `pnpm -w test`); the new sibling test file runs alongside the existing Clerk tests.
- The Clerk package typechecks cleanly — run `pnpm -w typecheck`.
- A well-formed mandate-shaped config (six states: `new`, `open`, `stuck`, `completed`, `failed`, `cancelled`, with the transition graph that mirrors the existing `ALLOWED_FROM` and a `childrenBehavior` declaring both `allSuccess` and `anyFailure` triggers) passes the validator without throwing.
- Each of the five brief-specified failure scenarios (unreachable `childrenBehavior` target from some non-terminal state; duplicate state names; empty/transition-less action on a declared trigger; terminal state with an outbound transition; well-formed config passing) has a corresponding test asserting the validator's behavior, and each failure-case test asserts the error message contains the offending field path.
- Each of the additional structural checks (referenced states must exist; non-initial states must have at least one inbound transition; exactly one initial state; non-empty type name) has at least one failing-case test.
- The validator throws on the *first* structural violation (no accumulated-error mode).
- The new types and the validator are importable from `@shardworks/clerk-apparatus` at the package's public entry point; no consumer needs to reach into an internal path.

## Existing Patterns

- **Canonical validator shape.** `packages/plugins/animator/src/rate-limit-backoff.ts::validateBackoffConfig` is the single closest precedent: pure function, structural checks layered top-down, plain-Error throws with the `[apparatus] path: problem; received value` message shape, accompanied by a sibling `.test.ts` with one happy-path plus one failing test per rejection mode. Follow this shape with no variation.
- **File layout precedent.** `packages/plugins/clerk/src/link-normalize.ts` and its sibling `link-normalize.test.ts` are the shape to mirror: one pure-function module co-located with its test, re-exported from the package barrel.
- **Kit-contribution validation idiom.** `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts::registerKitLinkKinds` shows how this codebase layers structural checks on plugin-contributed config entries and emits `[clerk] ...`-prefixed errors. Error-message voice should match.
- **Reachability implementation hint.** The all-reachability check (decision D11) is one forward-BFS per distinct transition target. Computing the forward-reachability closure once per starting state (or using a single-source reachability over the reversed graph from each target) is sufficient; a naive implementation nesting BFS inside a per-state loop works correctly but is slower than necessary. This is a correctness-preserving style note, not a requirement.
- **Existing state-machine vocabularies.** Read `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` (`WritPhase`, `WritCrawlResult`) and the state declarations in `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts` and `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/types.ts` for tone and naming — all three use string-literal unions. Stay consistent.

## What NOT To Do

- Do not modify `clerk.ts`, `types.ts`, or `guild-config.ts`. The refactor's other tasks own those files.
- Do not register the built-in mandate type. A later task owns that registration; duplicating the canonical mandate config here (for example as a shared export or a constant used outside the test file) pre-empts it. Inline the fixture in the test file only.
- Do not introduce a typed error class or a `Result`-style return. The validator throws plain `Error` objects and is `void`-returning on success.
- Do not add normalization, default-filling, or a "resolved config" return shape. The validator is pure validation.
- Do not enforce writ-type-`name` format rules (kebab-case, reserved-word lists, etc.). Non-empty is the only name-level check in this task.
- Do not detect cycles in the state graph. Mandate's `open ↔ stuck` is a legitimate cycle and rejecting cycles would break the canonical test case.
- Do not examine or modify `ClerkConfig.writTypes`. The guild.json-side `WritTypeEntry[]` shape is superseded by later work but is not this task's concern.
- Do not lift the new types or validator into a new shared workspace package. Colocation inside the Clerk package is the correct destination.
- Do not add JSDoc-deprecation markers to existing types or fields.

content.text:
Reporting findings. I've executed the reset, but uncovered a deeper issue.

## Status

- ✅ Implement engine reset from `running` → `pending`. DB backup at `/workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db.bak-coco-implement-unstick-20260424-2107`.
- ⚠️ Spider picked it up immediately — and the retry failed in 3ms with `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'path')`. It's now in retry-backoff (next attempt ~21:11). **It will keep failing in this loop** until the retry budget is exhausted, at which point the rig will fail.

## Root cause analysis

The rig is living on an old engine schema. Every engine in this rig stores `sessionId`, `startedAt`, and `yields` as **scalar fields on the engine object**, with `attempts: null`. Example from the DB:

```json
{
  "id": "draft",
  "status": "completed",
  "yields": { "path": "/workspace/vibers/tmp/nexus-drafts/nexus/...", ... },
  "attempts": null
}
```

Current framework code:

- `types.ts:45` declares: *"Scalar engine-level `startedAt`/`completedAt`/`error`/`sessionId`/`yields` fields do not exist — `attempts[-1]` is authoritative."*
- `tryCollect` reads sessionId via `latestAttempt(engine)?.sessionId` — so with `attempts: null`, the dead session was invisible to the collector. **That's why it was "hung."** Spider literally couldn't see the session.
- `buildUpstreamMap` reads `tail.yields` from `attempts[-1]` — so the scalar `yields` on every upstream completed engine is also ignored. When implement dispatches, `context.upstream['draft']` is `undefined`, hence `undefined.path` throws.

Since nine expensive engines already completed (three with anima sessions — reader-analyst, patron-anima, spec-writer), blowing away the rig means re-running all of them.

## Options

1. **One-shot migration of this rig.** I synthesize `attempts: [{ startedAt, sessionId, status: 'completed', yields }]` on each engine from its scalar fields, then re-reset implement to pending. All 9 prior engines' yields become readable via `attempts[-1]`, implement dispatches normally, downstream review/revise/seal can run. Scoped to this one rig; reversible via backup.
2. **Cancel the rig.** Clean, but throws away ~5 hours of work including three anima sessions. Spider will re-spawn a new rig from the writ.
3. **Framework patch (scalar-yields fallback).** Add `latestAttempt`/`buildUpstreamMap` fallback to scalar fields when `attempts` is absent. Fixes this rig and any others in the same state. But this is framework code — your call, and probably needs its own writ.
4. **Park for now.** Set a far-future `holdUntil` on implement so it stops retry-thrashing while you decide. No data changes.

**My recommendation: (4) park immediately** to stop the retry loop, then decide between (1)/(2)/(3). Migration (1) is the best value recovery if we trust the synthesis; framework patch (3) is the right systemic fix but can be done after we unblock this rig.

Also worth flagging: there are likely **other rigs in the same shape** — anything created before the schema flipped but still completing engines. If you want, I can sweep for them. 

What's your call?

content: Goal: unstick hung rig rig-mod69jd9-3d0c607f for the writ-type config writ. I reset the implement engine to pending, but it's now retry-thrashing on a schema-drift bug. Next: pick a path (park, migrate, cancel, or framework patch). (disable recaps in /config)

sqlite3 /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db "SELECT content FROM books_spider_rigs WHERE id = 'rig-mod69jd9-3d0c607f';" | jq '
.engines |= map(
  if .status == "completed" then
    # Synthesize attempts[] from scalar yields/sessionId/startedAt.
    . + {
      attempts: [
        (
          {startedAt: (.startedAt // "2026-04-24T00:00:00.000Z")}
          + (if .startedAt then {endedAt: .startedAt} else {} end)
          + {status: "completed"}
          + (if .sessionId then {sessionId: .sessionId} else {} end)
          + (if .yields != null then {yields: .yields} else {} end)
        )
      ]
    }
    | del(.sessionId) | del(.startedAt) | del(.yields)
  elif .id == "implement" then
    # Clean reset: no attempts, no scalars, no hold, no retry count.
    . + {status: "pending"}
    | del(.sessionId) | del(.startedAt) | del(.yields)
    | del(.attempts) | del(.attemptCount)
    | del(.holdUntil) | del(.holdReason) | del(.holdCondition) | del(.lastCheckedAt)
  else
    # pending engines downstream of implement — clean scalars just in case.
    del(.sessionId) | del(.startedAt) | del(.yields)
  end
)' > /tmp/rig-migrated.json && jq '.engines[] | {id, status, scalars: (has("yields") or has("sessionId") or has("startedAt")), attempts: (.attempts // null | if . then length else null end), yields_tail: (if .attempts then .attempts[-1].yields != null else null end)}' /tmp/rig-migrated.json
